subject: Physics
q: The Wheatstone bridge shown in the figure below, gets balanced when the carbon resistor used as R1 has the colour code (orange, red, brown). The resistors R2 and R4 are 80 Ω and 40 Ω, respectively. Assuming that the colour code for the carbon resistors gives their accurate values, the colour code for the carbon resistor, used as R3, would be
question: The Wheatstone bridge shown in the figure below, gets balanced when the carbon resistor used as R1 has the colour code (orange, red, brown). The resistors R2 and R4 are 80 Ω and 40 Ω, respectively. Assuming that the colour code for the carbon resistors gives their accurate values, the colour code for the carbon resistor, used as R3, would be
solution:
Colour band A and B indicate the first two significant figures of resistance in 'ohm', while the C band gives the decimal multiplier i.e., the number of zeros that follows the two significant figures A and B.

The last band D indicates the tolerance in percentage about the indicated value or in other word, it represents the percentage accuracy of the indicated value. 

For R1, the given colour of the rings of a carbon resistor are orange, red, brown then, the value of resistance is

R1=32×101

Condition for balance in the Wheat stone's bridge is

R1R3=R2R4

So, 320R3=8040

∴R3=160 Ω, so colour coding of resistor R3 is

Brown→1

Blue→6

Brown→1
16×101⇒160 Ω

subject: Physics
q: Two resistors are connected in series across a battery as shown in figure. If a voltmeter of resistance 2000 Ω  is used to measure the potential difference across 500 Ω resister, the reading of the voltmeter will be _____ V
question: Two resistors are connected in series across a battery as shown in figure. If a voltmeter of resistance 2000 Ω  is used to measure the potential difference across 500 Ω resister, the reading of the voltmeter will be _____ V
solution:
The voltmeter resistance is given to be 2000 Ω. The equivalent resistance in the given circuit is Req=500×20002000+500+600=1000 Ω.

Now, current in the circuit is i=201000 A.

Reading of the voltmeter will be, V=20-600×201000=8 V

Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>I</mo> </mrow> </math> is false, Statement-<math> <mrow> <mo>II</mo> </mrow> </math> is true.

solution:
For higher range resistance should be less,so statement I is false.

To increase the range by n-times additional shunt should be placed S=Gn-1 where 'G' resistance of galvanometer

After interchanging the resistances X and Y a new balance point is found at a distance l2 from end A. What are the values of X (in Ω) and l2?
solution:
For a balanced meter bridge, (y)×​39.5=x×100-39.5, 

x=12.5×39.560.5=8.16 Ω. When x and y are interchanged l 1 and ( 100− l 1 ) will also interchange, l2=60.5 cm.

subject: Physics
q: Ratio of thermal energy released in two resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> connected in parallel in an electric circuit is :
question: Ratio of thermal energy released in two resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></math> connected in parallel in an electric circuit is :
solution:
In parallel connection, potential difference across resistors remains the same.

Thermal energy released in resistor is given by H=V2R×t

So, the ratio is H1H2=V2tRV2t3R=3RR=3:1.

subject: Physics
q: Eight copper wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> and diameter d are joined in parallel to form a single composite conductor of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. If a single copper wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>l</mi></math> have the same resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>R</mi></mfenced></math> then its diameter will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>.
question: Eight copper wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> and diameter d are joined in parallel to form a single composite conductor of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. If a single copper wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>l</mi></math> have the same resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>R</mi></mfenced></math> then its diameter will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>.
solution:
Each wire has resistance, r=ρ4lπd2

Eight wire in parallel, then equivalent resistance is

R=r8=ρl2πd2

Single copper wire of length 2l has resistance R=ρ2l×4πd12.

Comparing both equations, we get,

ρ8lπd12=ρl2πd2

⇒d1=4d

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : For measuring the potential difference across a resistance of 600 Ω, the voltmeter with resistance 1000 Ω will be preferred over voltmeter with resistance 4000 Ω.

Reason R : Voltmeter with higher resistance will draw smaller current than voltmeter with lower resistance.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements : One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : For measuring the potential difference across a resistance of 600 Ω, the voltmeter with resistance 1000 Ω will be preferred over voltmeter with resistance 4000 Ω.

Reason R : Voltmeter with higher resistance will draw smaller current than voltmeter with lower resistance.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: A is not correct but R is correct
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is not correct
solution:
Voltmeter is connected in parallel, so it should have very high resistance as to measure the potential difference, all the current should pass through the resistor and no current should pass through voltmeter for precise readings.
The current will divide and travel through the voltmeter if the voltmeter has a low resistance and will then give faulty readings.

Thus, A is not correct but R is correct.

subject: Physics
q: From the combination of resistors with resistance values $R_1=R_2=R_3=5 \Omega$ and $R_4=10 \Omega$, which of the following combination is the best circuit to get an equivalent resistance of $6 \Omega$ ?
question: From the combination of resistors with resistance values $R_1=R_2=R_3=5 \Omega$ and $R_4=10 \Omega$, which of the following combination is the best circuit to get an equivalent resistance of $6 \Omega$ ?
solution: $\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}}=\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{15}=\frac{3+2}{30}=\frac{1}{6}$

subject: Physics
q:
A cylindrical conductor of length 2 m and area of cross-section $0.2 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$ carries an electric current of 1.6 A when its ends are connected to a 2 V battery. Mobility of electrons in the conductor is $\alpha \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / V. s$. The value of $\alpha$ is :
(electron concentration $=5 \times 10^{28} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and electron charge $\left.=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
question:
A cylindrical conductor of length 2 m and area of cross-section $0.2 \mathrm{~mm}^{2}$ carries an electric current of 1.6 A when its ends are connected to a 2 V battery. Mobility of electrons in the conductor is $\alpha \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^{2} / V. s$. The value of $\alpha$ is :
(electron concentration $=5 \times 10^{28} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ and electron charge $\left.=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right)$
solution:
For a cylindrical conductor with length 2 m, cross-section 0.2 mm² = 0.2 × 10⁻⁶ m², carrying 1.6 A at 2 V.
Resistance: $R = V/I = 2/1.6 = 1.25$ Ω.
Resistivity: $\rho = RA/L = 1.25 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-6}/2$
$= 0.125 \times 10^{-6}$ Ω·m.
Using $\rho = 1/(ne\mu)$, mobility: $\mu = 1/(ne\rho)$
$= 1/(5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.125 \times 10^{-6})$
$= 1/(1 \times 10^3) = 10^{-3}$ m²/V·s.
Thus $\alpha = 1$.

subject: Physics
q: The number of turns of the coil of a moving coil galvanometer is increased in order to increase current sensitivity by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The percentage change in voltage sensitivity of the galvanometer will be :
question: The number of turns of the coil of a moving coil galvanometer is increased in order to increase current sensitivity by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The percentage change in voltage sensitivity of the galvanometer will be :
solution:
Current sensitivity = Voltage sensitivity ×R

If current sensitivity is made 1.5 times, then R also increase 1.5 times.

Hence, voltage sensitivity =1.5× current sensitivity 1.5×R

= no change.

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to find emf of a cell using potentiometer, the length of null point for a cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If this cell is replaced by another cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>. the length-of null point increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: In an experiment to find emf of a cell using potentiometer, the length of null point for a cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If this cell is replaced by another cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math>. the length-of null point increases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
We know, the condition where the galvanometer shows a null point is,

E1E2=l1l2

So, 1.5E2=6060+40=610=35

⇒E2=52 V

Hence, the value of x=25.

q: Two sources of equal emfs are connected in series. This combination is connected to an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. The internal resistances of the two sources are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math>. If the potential difference across the source of internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is zero then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> will be
question: Two sources of equal emfs are connected in series. This combination is connected to an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. The internal resistances of the two sources are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>></mo><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math>. If the potential difference across the source of internal resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is zero then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> will be
solution:
Current through the wire will be,

I=2εR+r1+r2.

Now potential difference across cell 1 will be, ε-Ir1=0

⇒ε=2εR+r1+r2×r1⇒R+r1+r2=2r1⇒R=r1-r2

subject: Physics
q:
A 9 V battery with an internal resistance of 0.5 Ω is connected across an infinite network, as shown in the figure. All ammeters A1, A2, A3 and voltmeter V are ideal.
Choose the correct statement.
question:
A 9 V battery with an internal resistance of 0.5 Ω is connected across an infinite network, as shown in the figure. All ammeters A1, A2, A3 and voltmeter V are ideal.
Choose the correct statement.
solution:
In the given infinite network problems, let, X be an equivalent resistance. If we connect one section of an infinite network with the replacing network using an equivalent resistance, then in this new equivalent circuit the equivalent resistance will still remain the same.


Let, Req=x
x=4x4+x+2
x=8+6x4+x
4x+x2=8+6x
x2-2x-8=0. 

On solving,

x = 4 , x= -2
Here, the negative value of resistance is not possible.

So, resistance is x=4 Ω 

Reading of A1 is I=VX+r  ...(1), 

where, V=9 Volt, r = 0·5 Ω , R=4 Ω. 

Then, from the above equation,

I =94+0.5=2 A.

subject: Physics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> represents the actual number of deflections in a converted galvanometer of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> and shunt resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math>. Then the total current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> when its figure of merit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> will be
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> represents the actual number of deflections in a converted galvanometer of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> and shunt resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math>. Then the total current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> when its figure of merit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> will be
solution:
Let current through galvanometer be IG and current through shunt resistance be I-IG.

Given figure of merit K=IGn

Then, total current, I=IGG+SS

I=nKG+SS

q: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> amount of thermal energy is developed by a resistor in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through it. If the current is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the thermal energy developed by the resistor in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> will be
question: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> amount of thermal energy is developed by a resistor in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through it. If the current is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>, the thermal energy developed by the resistor in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> will be
solution:
Heat energy developed in the resistor due to current is given by, H=i2Rt. Therefore 

⇒H∝i2 (for t= constant)

Now required ratio, HH'=4162

⇒H'=16H

subject: Physics
q: A letter ${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}$ is constructed of a uniform wire with resistance $1.0 \Omega$ per $\mathrm{cm}$, The sides of the letter are $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the cross piece in the middle is $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ long. The apex angle is 60 . The resistance between the ends of the legs is close to:
question: A letter ${ }^{\prime} \mathrm{A}^{\prime}$ is constructed of a uniform wire with resistance $1.0 \Omega$ per $\mathrm{cm}$, The sides of the letter are $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the cross piece in the middle is $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ long. The apex angle is 60 . The resistance between the ends of the legs is close to:
solution: For $\mathrm{ADE} \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{x}}+\frac{1}{10}$ or $\quad R^{\prime}=\frac{20 x}{10+2 x}$ $$ \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\frac{20 \mathrm{x}}{10+2 \mathrm{x}}+20-\mathrm{x}+20-\mathrm{x} $$ or $\frac{20 \mathrm{x}}{10+2 \mathrm{x}}+40=2 \mathrm{x}$ Solving we get $$ \mathrm{x}=10 \Omega $$ Putting the value of $x=10 \Omega$ in equation (i) We get $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{BC}} & =\frac{20 \times 10}{10+2 \times 10}+20-10+20-10 \\ & =\frac{80}{3}=26.7 \Omega \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q:
A wire of resistance R is bent into an equilateral triangle and an identical wire is bent into $a$ square. The ratio of resistance between the two end points of an edge of the triangle to that of the square is
Options
question:
A wire of resistance R is bent into an equilateral triangle and an identical wire is bent into $a$ square. The ratio of resistance between the two end points of an edge of the triangle to that of the square is
Options
solution: For the wire bent into an equilateral triangle, each side has a resistance $\frac{R}{3}$. $R_{\text {eq }}=\frac{\left(\frac{2 R}{3}\right)\left(\frac{R}{3}\right)}{\frac{2 R}{3}+\frac{R}{3}}=\frac{2 R}{9}=R_1$ (lets say) For the wire bent into a square, each side has a resistance $\frac{R}{4}$. $\begin{aligned} & R_{\text {eq }}=\frac{\left(\frac{3 R}{4}\right)\left(\frac{R}{4}\right)}{\frac{3 R}{4}+\frac{R}{4}}=\frac{3 R}{16}=R_3 \text { (lets say) } \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{R_1}{R_3}=\frac{\frac{2 R}{9}}{\frac{3 R}{16}}=\frac{32}{27}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A potentiometer wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery and an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> in secondary circuit is balanced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> long potentiometer wire. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> (in volt) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A potentiometer wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected in series with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> battery and an external resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A cell of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> in secondary circuit is balanced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> long potentiometer wire. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> (in volt) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Since the given resistance is in series, so, total resistance R=20+30=50 Ω

Given,

Potential difference=25 V

Current I=VR=2550=0.5 A

Also, Potential gradient =VpdL=IRL

Where, Vpd=potential drop at wire

L=Length of wire

Potential gradient=20×0.510=1 V m-1

So, Emf of the cell is

E=Potential gradient×Balancing Length

E= 1× 250100

E= 2.5 V=2510 V

Thus, x=25.

q: A moving coil galvanometer has 150 equal divisions. Its current sensitivity is 10 divisions per milliampere and voltage sensitivity is 2 divisions per millivolt. In order that each division reads 1 volt, the resistance in ohms needed to be connected in series with the coil will be
question: A moving coil galvanometer has 150 equal divisions. Its current sensitivity is 10 divisions per milliampere and voltage sensitivity is 2 divisions per millivolt. In order that each division reads 1 volt, the resistance in ohms needed to be connected in series with the coil will be
solution:
$I_g=15 \mathrm{~mA} \quad V g=75 \mathrm{mV}$
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}_g}-\frac{\mathrm{V}_g}{I_g}$

q: A moving coil galvanometer allows a full scale current of <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> . A series resistance of <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is required to convert the galvanometer into a voltmeter of range <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> . Therefore, the value of shunt resistance required to convert the above galvanometer into an ammeter of range <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is:
question: A moving coil galvanometer allows a full scale current of <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> . A series resistance of <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is required to convert the galvanometer into a voltmeter of range <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> . Therefore, the value of shunt resistance required to convert the above galvanometer into an ammeter of range <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is:
solution:
ig=10-4 A

 i=10×10-3 A

 RV=20 kΩ

igG+ig RV=5

igG=i-igRA
RA=igGi-ig=ig5-igRVi-igig
RA=5-igRVi-ig≈300  Ω

subject: Physics
q: The resistance of hot tungsten filament is about 10 times the cold resistance. What will be the resistance of $100 \mathrm{~W}$ and $200 \mathrm{~V}$ lamp when not in use?
question: The resistance of hot tungsten filament is about 10 times the cold resistance. What will be the resistance of $100 \mathrm{~W}$ and $200 \mathrm{~V}$ lamp when not in use?
solution:
$R_{\text {hot }}=\frac{V^2}{P}=\frac{200 \times 200}{100}=400 \Omega$
cold resistance $=R_{\text {hot }} / 10=400 / 10=40 \Omega$

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and conductivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is subjected to an electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The expected value of current in the wire will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________.
question: A cylindrical wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and conductivity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is subjected to an electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The expected value of current in the wire will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mi>π</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________.
solution:
Conductivity σ=5×107 S m-1

Radius r=0.5 mm=5×10-4 m

E=10×10-3 V m-1

J=σE=10×10-3×5×107

⇒J=5×105

⇒iA=5×105

i=5×105×πr2

=5×105×π×5×10-42

=125π×10-3 A

Therefore, i=125 π mA

Hence, x=5

q: A galvanometer, whose resistance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi><mi>ohm</mi></math> , has <math><mn>25</mn></math> divisions in it. When a current of <math><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math> A passes through it, its needle (pointer) deflects by one division. To use this galvanometer as a voltmeter of range <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> it should be connected to a resistance of:
question: A galvanometer, whose resistance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi><mi>ohm</mi></math> , has <math><mn>25</mn></math> divisions in it. When a current of <math><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math> A passes through it, its needle (pointer) deflects by one division. To use this galvanometer as a voltmeter of range <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> it should be connected to a resistance of:
solution:
Given that the galvanometer has 25 divisions and the current required for deflection one division is 4×10-4 A

current for full scale deflection I=4×10-4×25 A
=10-2 A


To use this galvanometer as a voltmeter for a maximum voltage of  2.5 V, we need to add a resistance R in series to the resistance of the galvanometer of G = 50 Ω such that,

I = 2.5R+G⇒R = 2.510-2 - 50=200 ohm

subject: Physics
q: Two resistors of $200 \, \Omega$ and $400 \, \Omega$ are connected in series with a battery of $100$ V. A bulb rated at $200$ V, $100$ W is connected across the $400 \, \Omega$ resistance. The potential drop across the bulb is _______ V.
question: Two resistors of $200 \, \Omega$ and $400 \, \Omega$ are connected in series with a battery of $100$ V. A bulb rated at $200$ V, $100$ W is connected across the $400 \, \Omega$ resistance. The potential drop across the bulb is _______ V.
solution:
The resistance of the bulb is calculated using its power rating:

$R_b = \dfrac{V^2}{P} = \dfrac{200^2}{100} = 400 \, \Omega$

The bulb is connected in parallel with the $400 \, \Omega$ resistor. The equivalent resistance of this parallel combination is:

$R_p = \dfrac{400 \times 400}{400 + 400} = 200 \, \Omega$

This parallel combination is in series with the $200 \, \Omega$ resistor. The total equivalent resistance of the circuit is:

$R_{eq} = 200 + R_p = 200 + 200 = 400 \, \Omega$

The current drawn from the battery is:

$I = \dfrac{V}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{100}{400} = 0.25 \text{ A}$

The potential drop across the bulb is the same as the potential drop across the parallel combination:

$V_p = I \times R_p = 0.25 \times 200 = 50 \text{ V}$

Answer: $50$
